answer.text: An order that specifies the price at which the investor is willing to buy or sell, executed only when that price or better is available
An order that is executed immediately at whatever market price is available
An order that is automatically cancelled if not executed within one hour
An order that limits the maximum number of shares an investor can buy

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A limit order specifies the price at which the investor wants to trade. A buy limit order executes at the specified price or lower; a sell limit order executes at the specified price or higher. Unexecuted limit orders lapse at the end of the trading day. **The Trap:** Option (b) describes a market order, not a limit order. Limit orders provide price certainty but not execution certainty - the order may not get filled if the market doesn't reach the specified price. **Cross-Exam Context:** Limit orders are passive orders that add liquidity to the market. They sit in the order book until matched or cancelled. Price-time priority determines matching order. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Limit order = Price control, no execution guarantee. Market order = Execution guarantee, no price control. Choose based on whether price or speed matters more.
